content.text: is an AI system that conducts real telephone conversations over the public switched telephone network (PSTN) or VoIP infrastructure. The agent receives inbound calls (or places outbound ones), converts speech to text, reasons about what the caller wants, generates a response, and synthesises that response back into speech — all in near-real time, without a human in the loop for routine interactions.

Telephony providers such as Twilio and Vonage expose programmatic APIs that treat a phone call as a stream of audio. Your server connects over a WebSocket, receives raw audio frames, and sends audio frames back. Layered on top of that transport is a pipeline of three AI components: speech-to-text (STT), large language model (LLM), and text-to-speech (TTS). Together they replace a human operator for a well-defined category of calls.

Voice remains the primary channel for a large segment of customer interactions — appointment reminders, order status checks, insurance claims, utility outages, medical triage, and support escalations. Building a serviceable voice bot historically required expensive IVR (interactive voice response) platforms with rigid DTMF menus and scripted flows. Large language models remove the need to enumerate every possible path: the agent can handle paraphrasing, topic changes, and novel requests within a domain, which dramatically increases the completions rate without human handoff.

Outbound calling unlocks a complementary use case: proactive contact at scale. An agent can call hundreds of patients simultaneously to confirm appointments, or notify customers the moment their order ships, at a marginal cost orders of magnitude below a human call centre.
with no staffing cost. The agent handles calls at 3 am as well as during peak hours.
: the agent never has a bad day, never deviates from policy, and speaks at the same measured pace regardless of caller frustration.
: telephony APIs charge per minute, not per seat. Hundreds of simultaneous calls cost the same per-minute rate as one.
: every call generates a searchable, auditable record for QA, compliance, and training.
: the agent can transfer a call to a human agent when the situation requires empathy or authority, passing along the conversation summary.

: callers notice silence longer than 500–700 ms. Each pipeline stage (STT round-trip + LLM generation + TTS rendering) must fit within this budget; streaming all three in parallel is necessary, not optional.
: PSTN compression, background noise, speaker accents, and echo all degrade STT accuracy in ways that do not appear in benchmarks.
: recording consent disclosures, TCPA opt-in/opt-out for outbound calls, GDPR data retention limits, and state-specific wiretapping laws all require legal review before deployment.
: callers routinely speak over the agent. The system must detect that the caller has started speaking, stop TTS playback immediately, and restart the STT pipeline without dropping audio.
: the agent cannot verify caller identity beyond what it can ask. For sensitive actions (account changes, payment), a secondary channel or knowledge-based authentication is required.

When a call arrives, the telephony provider sends an HTTP POST to your webhook URL (a TwiML endpoint for Twilio). Your server responds with TwiML — a small XML document — that instructs Twilio how to handle the call. For a streaming agent, the key TwiML verb is

, which opens a WebSocket connection between Twilio's media gateway and your server, carrying bidirectional audio encoded as 8-bit μ-law (mulaw) at 8 kHz — the standard G.711 codec used across the PSTN.

Once the WebSocket is open, inbound audio arrives as base64-encoded mulaw chunks in JSON messages. Your server decodes these, converts them to 16-bit PCM, and feeds them to a streaming STT service such as Deepgram Nova-2 or Whisper. The STT service emits interim and final transcripts as the caller speaks. On a final transcript (or a configurable silence timeout), the transcript is sent to the LLM. The LLM streams its response token by token. As tokens arrive, they are buffered into short sentences, each sentence is converted to audio by the TTS service, and the resulting mulaw audio is streamed back over the same WebSocket. Twilio plays this audio to the caller in real time.
DTMF (touch-tone) digits arrive as separate WebSocket events and can be used for menu selection, PIN entry, or accessibility fallback when voice recognition fails.

attrs.alt: Phone call agent architecture: Caller (PSTN) to Twilio webhooks and media streams, WebSocket to Voice Agent Server (STT, LLM, TTS, state, tools), with recording branch and human transfer escalation path

content.text: The WebSocket carries two simultaneous streams: inbound audio (caller → Twilio → your server) and outbound audio (your server → Twilio → caller). Call recording is handled independently by Twilio's recording feature or by writing the raw audio to S3 from your server. Human escalation uses Twilio's
verb or a REST API call to
the active call to a SIP endpoint.
— medical practices, salons, service companies calling to confirm or rebook.
— e-commerce callers asking where their package is. The agent queries the order management system by tool call.
— collect account number, describe issue, check knowledge base, resolve or route to the right specialist.
— post-visit satisfaction calls, payment reminders (with TCPA-compliant opt-in), collection of structured data.
— replacing press-1-for-billing menus with a natural-language interface that reaches the right destination in a single spoken sentence.

The minimal stack is: a Twilio account with a purchased phone number, a Python or Node.js web server with a WebSocket handler, a streaming STT service (Deepgram is the lowest-latency hosted option), an LLM with streaming support, and a TTS service that can emit mulaw audio or PCM that you re-encode. For development, use the Twilio CLI's
Create a Twilio phone number and set its Voice webhook URL to your server's
In the WebSocket handler, decode incoming mulaw frames and stream them to Deepgram's real-time API.
transcript event, append the turn to the conversation history and call the LLM with streaming enabled.
Sentence-split the LLM stream, convert each sentence to mulaw via TTS, and send the audio back through the WebSocket as Twilio
events from Twilio's WebSocket message stream.
TwiML endpoint — set up the media stream

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, WebSocket
from fastapi.responses import Response

app = FastAPI()

WS_URL = "wss://your-server.example.com/ws"  # must be public HTTPS/WSS

@app.post("/twiml")
async def twiml_webhook(request: Request):
    """
    Twilio calls this endpoint when a call arrives.
    We respond with TwiML that opens a media stream (WebSocket)
    and plays an initial greeting while the WebSocket connects.
    """
    twiml = f"""
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Response>
  <Connect>
    <Stream url="{WS_URL}">
      <!-- Pass the call SID as a custom parameter so we can look up context -->
      <Parameter name="call_sid" value="{{{{CallSid}}}}"/>
    </Stream>
  </Connect>
</Response>"""
    return Response(content=twiml, media_type="application/xml")

import asyncio
import base64
import json
import audioop  # stdlib; converts between mulaw and PCM
import httpx
from fastapi import WebSocket
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

# ---- configuration ----
DEEPGRAM_API_KEY = "dg_..."
ELEVENLABS_API_KEY = "sk_..."
ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID = "21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM"  # Rachel
ANTHROPIC_CLIENT = AsyncAnthropic()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are a helpful phone agent for Acme Medical.
You can book, reschedule, or cancel appointments.
Be concise — callers are listening, not reading.
If you need to escalate, say \"Let me transfer you now.\" and nothing else.
"""


async def deepgram_stream(audio_queue: asyncio.Queue) -> asyncio.AsyncGenerator[str, None]:
    """Stream audio chunks to Deepgram and yield final transcripts."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        # Deepgram real-time streaming via HTTP chunked POST
        # (in production use the official deepgram-sdk WebSocket client)
        url = "https://api.deepgram.com/v1/listen?model=nova-2-phonecall&encoding=mulaw&sample_rate=8000&channels=1"
        headers = {"Authorization": f"Token {DEEPGRAM_API_KEY}"}

        async def audio_gen():
            while True:
                chunk = await audio_queue.get()
                if chunk is None:  # sentinel
                    break
                yield chunk

        async with client.stream("POST", url, headers=headers, content=audio_gen()) as resp:
            async for line in resp.aiter_lines():
                if not line:
                    continue
                data = json.loads(line)
                if data.get("is_final") and data.get("channel", {}).get("alternatives"):
                    yield data["channel"]["alternatives"][0]["transcript"]


async def text_to_mulaw(text: str) -> bytes:
    """Convert text to mulaw audio via ElevenLabs."""
    url = f"https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1/text-to-speech/{ELEVENLABS_VOICE_ID}/stream"
    headers = {"xi-api-key": ELEVENLABS_API_KEY, "Content-Type": "application/json"}
    payload = {
        "text": text,
        "model_id": "eleven_turbo_v2",
        "output_format": "ulaw_8000",  # native mulaw — no re-encoding needed
    }
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        async with client.stream("POST", url, headers=headers, json=payload) as resp:
            return await resp.aread()


async def send_audio_to_twilio(ws: WebSocket, stream_sid: str, mulaw_bytes: bytes):
    """Encode mulaw audio as base64 and send as a Twilio media message."""
    chunk_size = 160  # 20 ms of 8 kHz mulaw
    for i in range(0, len(mulaw_bytes), chunk_size):
        chunk = mulaw_bytes[i : i + chunk_size]
        await ws.send_json({
            "event": "media",
            "streamSid": stream_sid,
            "media": {"payload": base64.b64encode(chunk).decode()},
        })


@app.websocket("/ws")
async def call_websocket(ws: WebSocket):
    await ws.accept()
    stream_sid = None
    audio_queue: asyncio.Queue[bytes | None] = asyncio.Queue()
    conversation: list[dict] = []

    async def process_transcripts():
        """Background task: receive transcripts and generate LLM + TTS responses."""
        async for transcript in deepgram_stream(audio_queue):
            if not transcript.strip():
                continue
            conversation.append({"role": "user", "content": transcript})

            # Stream the LLM response sentence by sentence
            buffer = ""
            full_response = ""
            async with ANTHROPIC_CLIENT.messages.stream(
                model="claude-opus-4-5",
                max_tokens=200,
                system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
                messages=conversation,
            ) as stream:
                async for text in stream.text_stream:
                    buffer += text
                    full_response += text
                    # Flush on sentence boundaries for low latency
                    if any(buffer.endswith(p) for p in (".", "!", "?", "\n")) and len(buffer) > 20:
                        if stream_sid:
                            audio = await text_to_mulaw(buffer.strip())
                            await send_audio_to_twilio(ws, stream_sid, audio)
                        buffer = ""
            # Flush remainder
            if buffer.strip() and stream_sid:
                audio = await text_to_mulaw(buffer.strip())
                await send_audio_to_twilio(ws, stream_sid, audio)

            conversation.append({"role": "assistant", "content": full_response})

            # Check for escalation trigger
            if "Let me transfer you now" in full_response:
                # Call Twilio REST API to transfer the call to a human queue
                # (implementation omitted for brevity)
                break

    transcript_task = asyncio.create_task(process_transcripts())

    try:
        while True:
            raw = await ws.receive_text()
            msg = json.loads(raw)
            event = msg.get("event")

            if event == "connected":
                pass  # WebSocket handshake confirmed

            elif event == "start":
                stream_sid = msg["start"]["streamSid"]
                # Play a greeting immediately
                greeting = await text_to_mulaw("Hello, this is Acme Medical. How can I help you today?")
                await send_audio_to_twilio(ws, stream_sid, greeting)

            elif event == "media":
                # Decode base64 mulaw and enqueue for STT
                mulaw = base64.b64decode(msg["media"]["payload"])
                await audio_queue.put(mulaw)

            elif event == "dtmf":
                digit = msg["dtmf"]["digit"]
                # Treat DTMF as text input if caller uses touch-tone
                conversation.append({"role": "user", "content": f"[DTMF: {digit}]"})

            elif event == "stop":
                break
    finally:
        await audio_queue.put(None)  # signal STT task to finish
        transcript_task.cancel()
Outbound call — initiate via Twilio REST API

from twilio.rest import Client as TwilioClient

twilio = TwilioClient(account_sid="AC...", auth_token="...")

def place_outbound_call(to_number: str, from_number: str, patient_name: str) -> str:
    """
    Initiate an outbound call. Twilio will call `to_number`, and when
    the callee answers, Twilio fetches TwiML from the webhook URL.
    Pass custom parameters so the agent knows why it is calling.
    """
    call = twilio.calls.create(
        to=to_number,
        from_=from_number,
        url=f"https://your-server.example.com/twiml?patient={patient_name}&reason=reminder",
        method="POST",
        status_callback="https://your-server.example.com/call-status",
        status_callback_method="POST",
        status_callback_event=["completed", "no-answer", "busy", "failed"],
        machine_detection="DetectMessageEnd",  # skip voicemail
    )
    return call.sid


@app.post("/call-status")
async def call_status_webhook(request: Request):
    """Twilio posts call outcome here so we can log and retry if needed."""
    form = await request.form()
    call_sid = form.get("CallSid")
    status = form.get("CallStatus")
    answered_by = form.get("AnsweredBy")  # 'human' or 'machine_start'
    print(f"Call {call_sid} ended with status={status} answered_by={answered_by}")
    return Response(status_code=204)
A dental practice running 3,000 appointments per month used to employ two front-desk staff whose primary job was phone appointment reminders. After deploying a call agent:
The agent calls patients 48 hours before their appointment, confirms attendance, and offers to reschedule if the patient says they cannot make it.
The agent accesses the practice management system through a tool call to read available slots and writes a new booking directly.
Calls that turn into complaints or complex queries are transferred to a human via a
verb with a summary whispered to the agent before connection.
No-show rate dropped from 12 % to 4 % within the first two months. The two staff members were reassigned to in-chair patient experience roles.
Waiting for a complete LLM response before starting TTS
: this adds 1–3 seconds of silence. Stream the LLM and fire TTS on the first complete sentence immediately.
: the agent keeps talking while the caller is also talking. Track whether audio is being played and use Twilio's
message to stop outbound audio when inbound audio arrives.
Using a general-purpose LLM system prompt
: verbose, text-optimised responses are unnatural over the phone. Constrain responses to 1–2 sentences per turn in the system prompt.
: some TTS services return PCM or MP3. You must re-encode to 8 kHz mulaw before sending to Twilio; sending the wrong encoding produces static.
: in most US states, at least one party must consent to recording. The greeting must include the disclosure before any recording begins.
: a confused caller can keep the agent on the line indefinitely. Set a hard timeout (e.g. 10 minutes) and gracefully end calls that exceed it.
: the FTC requires that automated callers identify themselves as AI bots early in the call. Do this in the opening greeting.
parameter controls how long silence triggers a final transcript. Start with 500 ms for conversational calls; increase to 800 ms for slower speakers.
Use a dedicated STT model for telephony
is trained on 8 kHz PSTN audio and significantly outperforms general models on noisy calls.
but only flush when the sentence is at least 15 characters, avoiding TTS calls for short filler tokens.
: PSTN calls rarely run longer than a few minutes. Truncate history to the most recent 10 turns to keep latency stable.
Write a call summary on hang-up
event arrives, make one final LLM call to summarise the conversation and the outcome, then write it to your CRM.
End-to-end latency from the caller finishing a sentence to the first byte of the agent's audio response has three components:
: Deepgram streaming returns a final transcript within 200–300 ms of the last syllable on a clean PSTN line.
: Claude 3 Haiku and GPT-4o-mini return the first token in 150–400 ms. Larger models like Claude 3.5 Sonnet add 300–600 ms. Use a fast model for call agents.
: ElevenLabs Turbo starts streaming within 200–300 ms. The first sentence reaches the caller in about 150 ms of audio.

Total perceived latency: 600–1,200 ms end-to-end is achievable and acceptable. Above 1,500 ms callers start to wonder if the line dropped. Profile each stage separately in staging and optimise the bottleneck.

For scalability, your WebSocket server needs one coroutine per active call. An async Python server (FastAPI + uvicorn) can handle 500–1,000 concurrent calls on a single 4-core instance. Beyond that, run multiple instances behind a load balancer — but ensure each call's WebSocket lands on the same instance for the duration of the call (use Twilio's
TwiML + Media Streams WebSocket; largest ecosystem, best docs
WebSocket audio streams; good European coverage; NCCO JSON config
WebRTC gateway + SIP trunking; lower per-minute pricing for high volume
nova-2-phonecall model trained on PSTN; ~200 ms final transcript latency
WebSocket API; good accuracy; word-level timestamps
Low first-token latency; adequate for bounded call-centre domains
Highest naturalness; supports ulaw_8000 output; ~200 ms time-to-first-byte
AWS-native; synchronous and streaming modes; PCM output (re-encode to mulaw)
Open-source; handles barge-in, VAD, pipeline wiring; Twilio and Vonage plugins
Daily.co open-source framework; composable pipeline stages; strong Twilio support
Natural conversation; handles paraphrasing; no predefined menus
Higher latency than DTMF IVR; LLM cost; regulatory complexity
Complex domains where callers use varied language
Zero AI cost; deterministic; very low latency; simple to audit
Only handles menu-driven interactions; terrible caller experience; rigid
Simple routing (press 1 for sales) or accessibility fallback
More flexible than DTMF; predictable; lower cost than LLM
Intent taxonomy must cover all phrases; brittle for novel utterances
Narrow domains with known intents (e.g. flight check-in)
Handles any situation; empathy; can be authorized to take actions
Expensive per minute; limited hours; inconsistent quality
High-stakes or legally sensitive interactions; escalation target
Async; no latency pressure; easy to correct typos; cheap
Not all demographics use chat; some tasks require voice confirmation

endpoint is public. Without signature validation, anyone can trigger calls on your account. Twilio signs every webhook request with an HMAC-SHA1 of the URL and POST body using your auth token. Reject any request that fails validation:

from twilio.request_validator import RequestValidator
from fastapi import Request, HTTPException

VALIDATOR = RequestValidator(auth_token="your-twilio-auth-token")

async def verify_twilio_signature(request: Request) -> None:
    """Raise 403 if the request is not signed by Twilio."""
    url = str(request.url)
    signature = request.headers.get("X-Twilio-Signature", "")
    form = await request.form()
    params = dict(form)
    if not VALIDATOR.validate(url, params, signature):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=403, detail="Invalid Twilio signature")
: announce that the call may be recorded in the opening greeting, before recording begins, in every jurisdiction you operate in.
: for outbound calls, you must have written prior express consent before calling a mobile number with an automated dialling system. Maintain an opt-out list and honour
: call transcripts are personal data. Enforce data retention limits, support right-of-access requests, and encrypt transcripts at rest.
: the FTC's 2024 guidance and several US state laws require that AI systems identify themselves as such to callers. Build this into the greeting, not an afterthought.
: if the caller recites a credit card number, do not log the transcript containing the PAN. Detect card number patterns and redact them before writing to storage.
: if the STT WebSocket fails, fall back to DTMF-only mode rather than dropping the call.
: if the LLM returns an error or timeout, play an apology and transfer to a human instead of hanging up.
: Twilio will abort the call if the WebSocket handshake times out. Keep the server warm with a
endpoint and deploy with zero-downtime rolling restarts.
Telephony API → WebSocket → STT → LLM → TTS → audio back
TwiML verb is the standard entry point for this pipeline on the PSTN.
Latency is the primary UX constraint
. All three stages must stream concurrently. Start TTS on the first complete sentence from the LLM, not after the full response.
) — general ASR models are not trained on 8 kHz PSTN compression and will produce significantly more errors on real calls.
: recording disclosure, TCPA opt-in for outbound, GDPR retention limits, and AI identity disclosure must all be built into the system, not added later.
Human transfer is a first-class feature
, not an error handler. Design the escalation path early: what triggers it, how the summary is whispered to the agent, and whether the caller must wait on hold.
Framework choices exist for reducing boilerplate
: LiveKit Agents and Pipecat handle barge-in, VAD, and pipeline wiring; building from scratch is only worthwhile when you have unusual audio routing requirements.
Inbound and outbound share the same pipeline
once the WebSocket is open. The only difference is who initiates the call — Twilio's REST API for outbound versus an incoming webhook for inbound.
